name: Grand Challenges in Shape-Changing Interface Research
short_description: A CHI 2018 agenda paper that synthesizes 12 grand challenges to guide the maturing field of shape-changing interface research.
long_description: Authored by Alexander, Roudaut, Steimle, Hornbaek, Bruns Alonso, Follmer, and Merritt, this field-defining paper distills a workshop of 25 experts across design, computer science, HCI, engineering, robotics, and materials science into 12 grand challenges spanning technological, behavioural, and design concerns. Shape-changing interfaces use dynamic changes in a device's physical form as both input and output, making the body of the device itself a medium of interaction rather than a static enclosure for a screen. As a research agenda, it matters to physical AI and interaction design because it frames how dynamic physical embodiment, actuated materials, and morphing form factors should move from one-off prototypes toward coherent theory, evaluation, and ethics.
ai_note: This is an overview/agenda paper proposing research challenges; it contains no running AI/ML system, and shape-changing interfaces themselves are actuation and tangible-interaction techniques rather than AI.

images.caption: Figure from the CHI 2018 paper illustrating shape-changing interface research challenges.
images.alt_text: Diagram from an academic paper organizing grand challenges for shape-changing interface research across technological, behavioural, and design categories.

sources.title: Grand Challenges in Shape-Changing Interface Research

sources.title: Grand Challenges in Shape-Changing Interface Research (author PDF)
Grand Challenges in Shape-Changing Interface Research
sources.publisher: Bristol Interaction Group, University of Bristol

interactions.title: A phone that grows a game controller
interactions.description: The paper describes adaptive affordance, where a handheld device physically mutates its form to fit the task at hand — a flat phone bulging into a gamepad with ridges and grips when you launch a game, then flattening again afterward.

interactions.title: Curl the screen away from a stranger's gaze
interactions.description: As an example of adaptation to the user, the paper cites a device that changes its shape to hide screen content from a passerby — bending or shielding its surface so only the person holding it can see what's displayed on a crowded train.

interactions.title: A notification you feel, not hear, in a meeting
interactions.description: The authors point to quiet shape-changing notifications that avoid disrupting a meeting: instead of a buzz or chime, a handheld object subtly deforms in your palm to signal an incoming message, so you can read its urgency by touch without looking down.

interactions.title: Grab a bar chart and reshape the data
interactions.description: Under data physicalization, the paper describes pin-array displays such as inFORM where a dataset rises as a physical 3D bar chart you can reach into and manipulate by hand, exploiting lifelong haptic and visual perception rather than reading numbers off a flat screen.

interactions.title: Eyes-free navigation that nudges your hand
interactions.description: The paper notes shape-change can convey information haptically: a handheld device deforms to tell you which way to turn while walking a map route, letting you follow directions eyes-free with your phone still in your pocket or your eyes on the street.

interactions.title: Wearables and walls that move for their own sake
interactions.description: Among hedonic and symbolic uses, the paper points to shape-changing garments, actuated architecture, and works of art — clothing or building facades that morph for aesthetic, sensorial, or expressive pleasure rather than to complete a task.

interactions.title: A surface that firms up around an aching joint
interactions.description: Building on the paper's 'augment users' theme of smart fabric that changes stiffness around joints, a rehab sleeve could stiffen to gently restrict a recovering knee during one exercise and soften for free movement in the next — grounded in the actuated-materials techniques the agenda calls for.

interactions.title: Feel a molecule before you understand it
interactions.description: Extending the paper's 'simulate objects' purpose of exposing things humans could not normally grasp, a desktop pin-and-material display could let a chemistry student physically push and feel a protein fold, with the surface dynamically rendering both shape and hardness as in the integration-of-modalities challenge.
